policy_statement: By 2023, assemble data and develop metrics to inform strategic and equitable geographically focused electrification and gas decommissioning plans (ES.5-1).
mechanism_description: Assemble data and develop metrics to guide geographically focused electrification and gas decommissioning planning, enabling prioritized implementation that supports emissions reductions and equity goals.
primary_causal_pathway: Planning + coordination → accelerated electrification + gas network retirement → ↓ fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: governments/utility partners compile geospatial and equity-relevant data, set metrics, and create prioritized plans for where/when to electrify buildings and retire segments of the gas distribution system.  
(2) Mediating physical/process pathway: coordinated infrastructure and program sequencing reduces barriers and lock-in (e.g., avoids new gas hookups/replacements, targets circuits/areas for electric upgrades, enables safe gas main retirement), increasing the rate of fuel switching from methane combustion to electricity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: reduced on-site combustion CO₂/CH₄ emissions (and potentially reduced upstream methane leakage from the gas system) as electrification expands and the gas network shrinks.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling faster and broader replacement of fossil gas combustion with electrified end uses, resulting in lower direct GHG emissions from buildings/industry over time.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: governance planning enables electrification and gas decommissioning, which reduces fossil fuel combustion emissions—this is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while the action is “planning,” its intended and largest climate effect is emissions reduction (not primarily resource efficiency, adaptation, or ecosystem services).  
3) Edge cases applied: if electrification is supplied by high-carbon electricity in the near term, reductions may be delayed or smaller, but the mechanism remains aimed at decarbonization enabling; equity metrics are co-benefits rather than the dominant climate pathway.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy fitting this mechanism (data/metrics/planning for targeted electrification + gas retirement) is fundamentally designed to enable emissions cuts via fuel switching and system retirement, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., buildings vs. network leakage vs. implementation program design) is deferred to policy-level evidence and context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If plans are purely informational with no linkage to implementation, or if they primarily manage reliability/resilience without advancing electrification/gas retirement, the dominant effect could shift; also, net GHG impact depends on grid emissions intensity and actual follow-through on decommissioning.
key_indicators: “By 2023, assemble data”; “develop metrics”; “inform strategic and equitable planning”; “geographically focused electrification”; “gas decommissioning plans”; “metrics to inform prioritization and implementation”; “including cost, equity, safety, climate and just transition”
co_benefits: Equity; safety; just transition (workforce/community impacts); potential affordability/cost-management
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an information/disclosure or target-setting action due to “assemble data” and the deadline, but the dominant function is to support and structure planning/prioritization rather than public disclosure or a quantified performance target.
